era_context: The early 18th century, during the reign of Peter the Great in Russia (final years) and the beginning of the Yongzheng Emperor's reign in Qing China. Europe was in a period of relative peace following the War of Spanish Succession (1701–1713), though with significant diplomatic tensions and realignments. The Ottoman Empire remained a major Mediterranean and Middle Eastern power. European colonial expansion in the Americas, Africa, and Asia was accelerating. This was a period of intellectual ferment in Europe, with the Enlightenment beginning to take shape.

events.title: Yongzheng Emperor accedes to the throne of Qing China
events.description: On 20 December 1722 (in the Gregorian calendar; 11th month of the 61st year of the Kangxi reign in the Chinese calendar), the Kangxi Emperor died at the age of 68 after a reign of 61 years. His fourth son, who took the reign name Yongzheng, succeeded him. The succession was contested, as the Kangxi Emperor had not clearly designated an heir, and several of Yongzheng's brothers had claims to the throne. Yongzheng's accession was facilitated by his control of the imperial household and the support of key officials. The new emperor immediately began consolidating his power by purging rivals and implementing administrative reforms. Yongzheng's reign (1722–1735) would be marked by aggressive centralization of imperial authority and the suppression of factional disputes. The succession was controversial, and some historians have suggested that Yongzheng may have altered the Kangxi Emperor's will to ensure his own succession, though this remains disputed.

sources.name: Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty (Qing Shilu)

events.certainty_note: The Kangxi Emperor's death and Yongzheng's accession are well-documented in Qing state records. The date is certain in both the Chinese and Gregorian calendars.

events.title: Peter the Great conquers the Caucasian coast of the Caspian Sea
events.description: In 1722, Peter the Great launched a military campaign to conquer the Caucasian coast of the Caspian Sea, a region that had been under Persian control. The campaign, known as the Russo-Persian War (1722–1723), was motivated by Peter's desire to expand Russian territory and to secure access to trade routes in the Caucasus and Central Asia. Russian forces captured several key cities, including Derbent and Baku. The conquest was facilitated by the weakness of the Persian Safavid Empire, which was experiencing internal instability. However, the conquest was short-lived, as Peter's death in 1725 and subsequent Russian military commitments led to the abandonment of most of the conquered territories by 1732. The campaign is significant as one of Peter's final military achievements and as an early example of Russian imperial expansion into the Caucasus.
key_figures: Peter the Great (Tsar and Emperor of Russia)
Shah Sultan Husayn (Safavid Shah of Persia)
events.certainty_note: Peter the Great's conquest of the Caucasian coast is well-documented in Russian military records and contemporary accounts.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor dies; end of a 61-year reign
events.description: On 20 December 1722 (in the Gregorian calendar), the Kangxi Emperor died at the age of 68 after a reign of 61 years (1661–1722). The Kangxi Emperor had been one of the greatest rulers of the Qing Dynasty and of Chinese history. His reign was marked by military conquests, administrative reforms, and cultural achievements. The Kangxi Emperor had expanded Qing territory to include Taiwan, Mongolia, and Tibet. He had also promoted Confucian learning and the arts, and his reign is often seen as a golden age of Qing culture. The Kangxi Emperor's death marked the end of an era in Chinese history. His successor, the Yongzheng Emperor, would continue many of his policies but would also implement more autocratic and centralized forms of governance.

sources.name: Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty (Qing Shilu)

events.certainty_note: The Kangxi Emperor's death date is well-documented in Qing state records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Russo-Persian War begins; Peter the Great invades Persia
events.description: In 1722, Peter the Great launched the Russo-Persian War (1722–1723), invading the Safavid Persian Empire to conquer the Caucasian coast of the Caspian Sea. The war was motivated by Peter's desire to expand Russian territory and to secure access to trade routes. Russian forces captured several key cities, including Derbent and Baku. The conquest was facilitated by the weakness of the Persian Safavid Empire, which was experiencing internal instability and civil war. However, the conquest was short-lived, as Peter's death in 1725 and subsequent Russian military commitments led to the abandonment of most of the conquered territories by 1732. The Russo-Persian War is significant as one of Peter's final military achievements and as an early example of Russian imperial expansion into the Caucasus and Central Asia.
key_figures: Peter the Great (Tsar and Emperor of Russia)
Shah Sultan Husayn (Safavid Shah of Persia)
events.certainty_note: The Russo-Persian War is well-documented in Russian military records and contemporary accounts.

events.title: Defoe publishes Moll Flanders; early English novel
events.region: Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland
events.description: In 1722, Daniel Defoe published Moll Flanders, a novel presented as the autobiography of a woman who had lived a life of crime and prostitution. The novel was one of the earliest examples of the English novel form and is considered a landmark in the development of the novel as a literary genre. Moll Flanders was written in the first person and presented a realistic portrayal of the life of a lower-class woman in England. The novel was controversial for its frank treatment of sexuality and crime, but it was also popular with readers. Defoe's novel is significant as an early example of the novel form and as a work that challenged conventional morality and social norms. The publication of Moll Flanders in 1722 was part of a broader development of the novel form in early 18th-century England.
events.certainty_note: The publication date of Moll Flanders is well-documented in publishing records and contemporary literary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The succession of the Yongzheng Emperor (1722-001) has been the subject of historical controversy. Some historians have suggested that Yongzheng may have altered the Kangxi Emperor's will to ensure his own succession, though this remains disputed and is not supported by strong evidence. The traditional narrative presents Yongzheng's accession as legitimate, but some scholars have questioned whether the Kangxi Emperor truly intended Yongzheng to be his successor. Additionally, the significance of Peter the Great's conquest of the Caucasian coast (1722-002, 1722-004) has been reassessed by modern historians. While it is often presented as a major achievement, some scholars argue that it was less significant than once believed, as the conquest was short-lived and did not result in lasting Russian control of the region. The conquest is also sometimes presented as a precursor to later Russian imperial expansion, but this teleological interpretation may overstate its historical importance.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1722 is rich, particularly for European and East Asian events. Chinese imperial records are comprehensive, and Russian military records are well-preserved. European literary and cultural records are also abundant. However, the record is heavily weighted toward elite political, military, and cultural history. The perspectives of common people, merchants, and non-elite populations are underrepresented. The Chinese sources are official and may downplay internal problems or dissent. The Russian sources reflect the perspective of the centralizing state. Persian sources on the Russo-Persian War are limited, and the conflict is primarily documented from the Russian perspective. The literary record is dominated by European contributions; non-European literature is largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Yongzheng's accession in 1722 marked the beginning of his reign (1722–1735)
Peter's conquest of the Caucasian coast in 1722 led to the Treaty of Constantinople in 1723
The Kangxi Emperor's death in 1722 led directly to Yongzheng's accession
The Russo-Persian War (1722–1723) was concluded by the Treaty of Constantinople
Defoe's Moll Flanders (1722) was contemporary with Swift's Gulliver's Travels (1726), both landmarks in the development of the English novel
